description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 5, part 23: 'Snoop went some place'

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-66.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 108 hours, 16 minutes into the flight of Apollo 10.
We are now about 50 seconds from reacquiring the spacecraft, now in its seventeenth revolution of the Moon.
We expect when next we hear from the crew that Tom Stafford and Gene Cernan will be back in the LM - rather back in the Command Module.
We'll, shortly after acquisition, be scheduled to separate the LM followed by the unmanned ascent stage burn to depletion, and we hope to have television coverage, of that event.
Coming up on 15 seconds now to Acquisition Of Signal.
We'll be standing by for that.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: LM Telcom reports that we have good signal strength on the High Gain Antenna.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We read you loud and clear.
How are things going?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Well, we're all back in the Command Module, the tunnel's all locked up, and we're in attitude, and standing by to Sep here when you give us the word.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
We're looking good for Sep here, now, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay, Joe.
Now again that tunnel won't vent, so what we've done is pumped our cabin pressure up about 4 psi above it - four-tenths - and we're holding real good.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The crew have over pressurized the CM cabin to 5.4 psia to prove they have a good seal on the CM forward hatch.
Any leak would become apparent in a change of the docking tunnel to CM Delta-P value.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
Understand, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay, Now, what attitude do you wish us to go to when we - after we separate.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
Charlie Brown, stand by just 1.
I'll get you that.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We'll get you some gimbal angles for that attitude after Sep.
In the meantime, we'd like for you to, on your B Cryo H2heaters: on tank 1, go to Auto; and on tank 2, go to Off, please.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
Now do we have a Go for Pyro Arm, here?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We're standing by for Logic.
We'll give you a Go on the Pyro Arm here in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I got the Logic off.
You want me to turn it on?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, Charlie Brown.
Go ahead and turn it on.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The SECS Logic breakers on panel 8 must be closed, and switches also on panel 8, in the Logic position so that MCC-H can verify that the power has been connected on logic buses A&B before the pyros are armed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, We're armed.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We got your switches on, now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
Your gimbal angles for attitude after Sep are: roll, 180; pitch, 252; and yaw, three balls.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
Roll, 180; pitch, 252; and yaw is all balls.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And when do you want us to separate, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
Want - We can go ahead and separate now.
Charlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Houston.
We'll give you a countdown.
We're all set to go for Sep.
Right?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's affirmative, Charlie Brown.
We're standing by for your count.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Give you a five count.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Fire.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The final switch to be thrown to jettison the LM and the docking collar is the CSM/LM Final switch 1 or 2 on panel 2.
Either switch 1 or 2 can be used.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The switch is a spring loaded toggle switch, which is held in the down position to trigger detonation of the explosive ring charge that runs around the docking collar to sever it from the CM docking tunnel forward bulkhead.
See the diagrams below.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 16-mm film of the LMSnoopyjettison with synchronised audio.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: H.264 MOV video file.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cabin pressure's holding.
Snoop went some place.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, Charlie Brown.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, Charlie Brown.
Go.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Man, when he leaves, he leaves.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yeah.
Okay.
Don't back into that dude, now, John, when you get turned around.
Are you keeping it in sight?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
Okay.
Joe, he took off so fast, he's gone; he went right into the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Copy.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We don't have any idea where he went.
He just went boom and disappeared right into the Sun.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If you give us gimbal angles and allow us to burn out of here, we'll be okay.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Charlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, Charlie Brown.
Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Look, let's take a quick look at these orbital mechanics.
When we separated Orb Rate, he was straight up, and he had that 5 psi on the tunnel; and he took off like a scalded rock, straight up.
Okay?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger that.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Now, if we go to this attitude, do you want us to - Okay.
Now when we go to this attitude we're going to be looking down local vertical.
All right?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Now he's up above us some place, and I don't know where.
Now, do you want us to thrust down?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
Stand by, Charlie Brown.
We're running this thing through right now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yeah.
Because we don't want to see Snoopy come back here with a full head of steam.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's not nothing you have to make an immediate decision about.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You still have plenty of time.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Snoopy - Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yeah.
I hope this is Houston.
We're going to try to pick Snoop up on our VHF ranging, but go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay [laughing].
You didn't leave anybody in there did you, Tom?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: No.
I don't think so.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
This is Houston again.

Tom, what we want you to do is to - in the attitude that you're going to now, this attitude we passed up to you We want you to burn plus-X instead of minus-X, and this should give you separation based on this velocity, which we think is due to that pressure in the tunnel; and, also, we want you to Enable Bravo 3 and Charlie 4 jets.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Bravo 3 and Charlie 4.
Okay.
Now this is what I thought, too.

But when we separated that attitude, Snoop took off in a vertical climb like mad so were going down and out in front of him, and so if we go down and thrust plus-X, that'll take us down even further and out in front.
Over.
Does your FIDO agree with that?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I think they do, Tom.
They're kind of scratching their heads right, now.
Roger on that.
And the reason that Snoopy took off were showing that he vented all the pressure out of the cabin.
We think that vented out through the tunnel, and that probably gave him some Delta-V.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: During the LM ascent stage separation from the Command Module, the Lunar Module cabin pressure dropped rapidly, as measured by three separate transducers.
Telemetry data were lost for 12 seconds beginning at the initiation of separation.
The cabin pressure was 4.86 psia at the initiation of separation and 0.70 psia at the end of the telemetry dropout and continued to decay slowly.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Motion pictures of the final separation were taken from the Command Module.
A brown material, shown projecting from the tunnel and flapping, was the insulation around the Command Module docking ring.
The Lunar Module hatch was closed in the first frame in which it was visible.
This frame was taken 2 seconds after initiation of separation.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The film was used for determining a history of relative separation distance between the Command Module and the Lunar Module.
A time history of relative acceleration, or the required pressure force, was then estimated from the data.
The maximum acceleration of 50 ft/sec/sec (15 m/sec/sec) shown is considerably in excess of the acceleration caused by separation pyrotechnic effects.
However, the acceleration history can be obtained by dumping the cabin pressure in the first 0.3 second of separation.
A 4-psi drop in cabin pressure in 0.3 second requires a constant venting area of 290 square inches (0.187 m2).

The impulse from dumping cabin pressure through the hatch is consistent with the direction and magnitude of the Lunar Module velocity change (5 ft/sec in minus X direction, 1.5 m/s) noted from the flight data.
Further, the upper hatch is the only item on top of the Lunar Module that could open and close, allowing the cabin to vent while satisfying the pressure history.
The hatch has a maximum area of 838 square inches (0.54 m2), which is more than enough to vent the cabin from 4.8 to less than 1.0 psia in 0.3 second.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The events postulated to vent the cabin are as follows.
The hatch differential pressure resulting from the pyrotechnic firing broke the hatch latch and allowed the cabin to vent through the docking tunnel.
The outflow closed the hatch 0.3 second after separation but did not seal it completely.
The remaining gap of about 1.4 square inches (9.03 cm2) allowed a slight pressure decrease, as indicated by cabin pressure data.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The hatch and latch assembly was statically pressure-tested to failure.
At a differential pressure of 4.1 psi, the latch failed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On Apollo 9, the cabin pressure was maintained after separation.
The only difference between Apollo 9 and 10 was that the Apollo 10 tunnel could not be vented because the vent line was blocked.

At the time the separation pyrotechnics were fired on Apollo 9, the tunnel pressure was less than the Lunar Module cabin pressure; thus, the dynamic pressure in the tunnel was not sufficient to fail the hatch latch.
On Apollo 10, with the tunnel pressurized to 4.86 psia, the differential pressure when the pyrotechnics were fired was enough to fail the latch.
In summary, the analyses indicate that the loading on the Lunar Module hatch at separation exceeded the capability of the latch.
The hatch then opened and closed resulting in a separation distance and relative accelerations as shown in figures below.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is now concerned that having lost sight of the LM Snoopy ascent stage, he wants to be sure not to re-rendezvous with the LM.
The Flight Plan calls for the separation manoeuvre to be away from the centre of the Moon.
This was the vector that the LM ascent stage disappeared along, so Stafford understandably is reluctant to follow in the same direction.
MCC-H, having considered the dynamics of the LM jettison, agrees with Stafford's analysis, and amends the separation manoeuvre to down toward the centre of the Moon.
This will require the CSM Charlie Brown RCS to burn upward, away from the Moon, lowering the CSM orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We had the hatch valve in Auto, set right and everything.
Everything was squared away for Snoop.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We may have some problem with some of that stuff sticking in valves, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Well, I would believe that.
It was like a snow storm when Snoop took off [laughing].
You wouldn't believe it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Laughter] I bet that's right.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And it was right into the Sun, babe, right into the Sun.
How soon do you want to do this burn?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We want to sit tight for a little while here.
We got about another 20 minutes before we want to burn.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We're firming up all of these things on attitudes and burns for you.
We want to make sure we've got everything right before we torch off Snoopy.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yeah.
Thank you.
I think we'll be in good shape thrusting down, but that initial callout looks like we were just trying to make another high sight on him if we were going to use minus-X.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Laughter.] Okay.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: It's really impossible to hit him if you aim to hit him, anyway.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes, sir.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
Charlie Brown, while these troops are getting all their numbers all confirmed here, let me pass up some other data to you.
Weve got a new sleep attitude we want you to go to.
This is to cool [RCS] quad A; and the attitude roll is 090; pitch, 210; yaw, 000.
And in that attitude, we'd like the High Gain Antenna, pitch to minus 5, yaw to 231.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
And this sleep attitude is: roll, 090; pitch, 210; yaw, three balls; and High Gain: pitch, minus 5; and yaw, 231.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
That's right Gene-o.
In addition, tonight we'd like a waste-water dump, and we'd like it at your convenience; that can be at any time.
Down to 25 percent again.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay, Joe.
Almost everything, including going to bed, is going to have to be at our convenience before we get out of suits and things.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes.
Okay.
And, did you happen to notice the docking angle when you came back through the tunnel; and, also, did you get that big old canister back on board?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes, Joe.
We got the canister on board and John greased it in again.
The roll angle was plus one-tenth.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
Plus one-tenth; that's pretty darn good.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You don't believe that do you, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I believe that, John.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: It's the guy - It's the guy that aligned it that made it that way.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I don't believe that, John.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: It's got a sliding scale in the tunnel; we put it anywhere - It's got a sliding scale in the tunnel; we put it anywhere we want to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That I believe.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And, Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
In your configuration - In your sleep configuration, we want you to Disable C and D quads with the Auto RCS Select; in the DAP we want you to fail C and D and select AC roll in DAP.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And, Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We want you to go ahead and initiate your plus-X, 2-foot per second in X, now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
Okay.
I'll get that quad stuff back here after we do this, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That will be fine.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Charlie Brown?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Hang on just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Go ahead.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
We want you to enable all quads: that will be five 1's in your DAP.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
We got 2.1 on plus-X, here.
Read our DSKY: X is 2.1, Y is 0.1, and Z is minus 0.1 [fps].
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Tom.
We copy; and verify on the...
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And 1.9 [fps, 0.58 m/s] on the EMS.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And 1.9 on the EMS.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We show you separating, and we'll keep you posted on countdown on the ignition.
In the meantime, I've got a map update to send to you and also, some data for your photography when you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
Look, we're kind of bushed right now, and we don't need any more photography for today, Joe.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.

It's going to take us a couple of hours to get out of the suits and to get all the spacecraft squared away and all the stowage squared away, and we've had a long day, so we want to - and we got a lot of landmark tracking, to do tomorrow, so we'd just like to call it quits.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Yes.
We're still set up to do the contingency EVA.
We've got the couches stowed and everything.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Prior to the LM separation, Young configured the CM cabin for a potential contingency EVA, should they not be able to redock or use the docking tunnel for crew transfer between the two spacecraft.
This preparation involved stowing the crew couches and installing guard rails on the main display panels to facilitate crew ingressing the CM through the main hatch, in pressurized PGAs, in a vacuum.
This equipment needs to be removed and the cabin reconfigured for re-occupation by all three crew members.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young mentioned how busy he had been during the solo operations during the post mission crew debriefing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"I'd like to say something about that entire day.
That entire day, I had time to eat a couple of glasses of juice in the morning, no lunch, and a couple of pieces of food at night.
That is all the time there was for eating.
There wasn't any time during the rendezvous, and that is just the way it was.
But I wasn't particularly hungry because we were so bloated."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young (continued):"One of the major problems that we encountered in the Command Module during the entire day, as far as operations were concerned, was moving around in the pressure suit with those hoses on.
It was a continual struggle from the left seat to the Lower Equipment Bay and also over to the right seat to perform periodic systems checks.
It seemed like I was always fighting the hoses.
Finally, I got used to it and found that it would be much easier if you just took things slow and easy.
You'd get things done a lot faster than if you got all excited about tangling up in your hoses.
You had to continually pay attention to avoid being wrapped up in your hoses and getting all tangled up.
It was our intention to lower the right X-X strut.

In the simulator, we found that we could not operate in the spacecraft with the hoses on the right X-X strut attached because, with the spacecraft prepared for the contingency EVA, it took much too long to get in and out of the couch and go back and forth.
But, in zero gravity, it was simple matter to slide in and out of the left couch and slowly push your way down to the Lower Equipment Bay without disconnecting the X-X strut.
I'd recommend that as little of the spacecraft be taken apart as possible.
The preparation for the contingency EVA was accomplished in an estimated 10 minutes.
It's a very simple matter to unstow and to uninstall and stow the center couch and to make the associated changes, such as installing suit hose in it and making such changes to the hatch.
It's all listed in our checklist.
It would have taken us an additional 10 minutes or so to do the rest of the changes which consisted of completely venting the hatch and removing the pit pin to do the contingency EVA."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
We concur on that.

One item I want to send up to you though, in case you start to charge batteries in the morning before we get in contact with you, we're going to start charging A instead of B, but we'll give you a call first thing, and when you wake up.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'll wait to talk to you in the morning before I do that.
huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That will be fine, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I wish they'd have...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And anytime tonight if one or the other of those quads starts to heat up and we need to change the attitude, for crying out loud, call us and tell us.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
We sure will, John.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Houston, this is Charlie Brown here.
What's the analysis on that quad A; are we near the fracture mechanics limits?
It looks like it may be starting to cool off a little bit on the gauge and come down maybe to about 390 degrees (°F, 199°C).
Could you give us a quick synopsis?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We sure will.
Just a minute, Tom; I'll get it.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Houston, this is Charlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Go ahead, Charlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'm glad they don't put them days one on top of each other, I'll tell you that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Boy, you guys had a real one today, but you sure did good work.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: That's not a bad day's work for four and a quarter, is it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And those machines have been doing the work.
They really were slick.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Yes.
And we also had a lot of good help from you down on the ground, and we sure appreciate it.
I thought the total system and everything what the flight was for to test the system turned out real well.
We still had some rough spots and some comms and a few other things, but, by and large, the whole system pulled it off.
And it made us real happy, but needless to say were a little bit tired tonight.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Well, we can sure understand that, Tom, and we concur with everything you say.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Hey, Joe, tell - I'll buy a super FIDO and super GUIDO a beer for the CSI burn.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger that.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: This is Mission Control...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And our new model in this thing is really a slicky, boy.
It knows right where it's going all the time.
Both those - all those solutions were just - shoot, you could just flip the coin and picked any of them.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We're assuming, based on Tom Stafford's remark that the crew is quite tired, that we will not be getting the TV transmission that had been scheduled during the APS burn to depletion.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay.
Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
We show you about 2,000 feet [600 meters] separation on Snoopy, now, and we'll keep you posted on - on our ignition.
And on this quad A, we're showing about 129 [°F, 54°C] temperature - package temperature on the ground here, Tom.
Did you say you're reading 390?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Not the package temperature, the helium tank temperature.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
Copy.
Helium tank.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay, Charlie Brown.
This is Houston.
We've had ullage; armed the engine.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Where is it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We got ignition on Snoopy, Charlie Brown.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: The LM Snoopy APS burn to depletion ignition occurred at 108:52:05.5 and the burn was completed at 108:56:14.5; total Delta-V, 3,713.4 fps (1,131.8 m/s), placing the ascent stage in solar orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, I may see it out there; I'm not sure but I think I do.
I do!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Very good.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'll see if I can tell you when he burns out.
That's a long burn, though, isn't it?
4 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes, Can you tell which way he's going?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Babe, it's just fire to me; I think he's going up, but see, I'm not rightsideup either, but...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: He's going, Joe.
As long as I can see the fire, I guess he's going the other way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
From down here he looks like he's doing real good, Gene-o.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Joe, would he be burning away from us, sort of like maybe his attitude is local horizontal or close to it?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
That's affirmative.
He should be going in that direction.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
I got him out my right-hand window here; he's getting smaller, and he's still on fire.
How much more burn time has he got?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stand by, and I'll find out.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Hey, he just went out.
It just went out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We've got him still burning...
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Maybe it's because the Sun went down.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes.
Maybe.
We got him still burning Gene-o, and about 40 seconds of burn time yet.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Maybe I - that looked like him; maybe it wasn't.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Deke says he thinks he may have turned around and probably burning back at you now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: [Garble] No, I fixed those switches so he couldn't do that.
I'm glad to see that he's burning; that I didn't screw up or something in there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'm trying - I'm trying to remember now what I forgot in there - what I left in there, my helmet or something.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The way he took off...
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: [Garble] do you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...it doesn't look like you left very much in there at all.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Man, we had PLSSs and probes and drogues and all sorts of things on there.
How far will you be able to track him?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Probably for several hours.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Is he really going to the Sun?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, he's going in that general direction.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: God, I feel sort of bad about that, because he's a pretty nice guy; he treated us pretty well today.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
That's affirm.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: That's what I talk about using up a piece of hardware, though, aint it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger that.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We show that we achieved a velocity of about 3,700 feet per second [1,128 m/s] from that burn.
The nominal burn time would have been around 3 minutes 34 seconds and we'll have some refinements to that later.

In order to conclude the change of shift press conference that was in progress when this pass began, we will take the circuit down and record any subsequent communications with the spacecraft and play those back to you following the completion of the change of shift press conference.
This is Apollo Control at 108 hours, 58 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, Charlie Brown.
This is Houston, Snoopy did a real good job burning, and we're still tracking him real good.
Getting good data from him, and we're going to let you go ahead and start through your pre-sleep checklist, and I'll try to keep the calls to a minimum until just prior to LOS.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
Joe, should we go to sleep attitude now?
Is that okay with you?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Stand by, Charlie Brown.
I'm trying to find out now.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Charlie Brown, Houston.
Roger.
You can go ahead and go into your sleep attitude any time you want to, now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Apollo 10, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Go ahead.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger, 10.
That was a beautiful job today.
If you do half that well tomorrow, we'll let you come home.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: We'll do better than that tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Thank you, Deke.
We'll probably be ready by then, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes.
Get a good night's sleep; you can use it.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-67.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Actually, like Tom said, there's a lot of people who did a good job, and, I'll tell you, these vehicles, so far - That little Snoopy was a real winner.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We concur.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: And big Charlie Brown - And big Charlie Brown is no slouch either.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
I hate to bother you, but if you'll give us the computer, we'd like to update your state vector.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
You want it in the middle of this maneuver, or can you wait until we finish, or not?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We can wait till you finish, John.
My error.
I didn't notice you were maneuvering there.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
I don't think...
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
You have the computer.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Apollo 10, ready.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: P00 and Accept.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Apollo 10.
Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Apollo 10, did you get our Roger?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I got it twice.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's an interesting point about the communications, today.
Sometimes I was hearing myself speak and, also, Gene and Tom speak twice.
I don't understand all that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm not so sure either, John, unless maybe we were getting some relay modes in there today, and I think - yes, that comm guys are nodding their heads yes - We were getting some relay modes, where probably you were coming down to the ground and getting fed back to yourself.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Oh.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Let me give you one instance, that I think I know that happened: When Charlie was on, and you weren't able to reach Snoopy, and in order to read him, Charlie would key his mike down here.

What would happen, is you'd come down to the ground - you'd come down to us and back up to Snoopy, but you'd hear yourself coming back up with about a 3-second delay; and it probably sounded like a pretty good echo.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Well, I understand that.
That's a good capability to have, to be able to ground relay like that.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And, Charlie Brown, this is Houston.

I guess when you have these little intermittent times when you were hearing yourself talk, that probably was the result of this same configuration being brought up inadvertently, when maybe you were trying to talk to Snoopy, and we didn't know it at the time and tried to make a transmission and were keyed for a few seconds.
We would do the same thing; you'd relay down here and back to yourself for a short period of time there.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston, We're through with the computer now; you can go back to Block.
It's all yours for the night.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Go ahead, 10.
Houston.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
Could you review this DAP configuration one more time that you want us to be in?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
Stand by.
Ill make sure I've got it right before I pass it up to you, John.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, John.
On your DAPs, if you'll make R2 read 11100, then you'll have the DAP in the right configuration.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: This DAP code in register 2 breaks down as - Use quads A&C for roll control, quad A operational, quad B operational, quad C as failed, quad D failed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
Outstanding.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And, also, Charlie Brown, on your quad A, we're showing less than 100 degrees [°F, 37°C] right now on the temperature and going, down, so we feel that there's no problem on that over temperature on the con - on that quad.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Go ahead.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay.
I just wanted to hit you with a couple of things before you go around the corner.
First off, looks like you've got a real good, tight cabin there, in case there's any doubt in your mind of the stuff being in the seal.

What we're looking at right now, and kind of thinking about, and going to let you think about it on this pass, and then pick you up if you're still awake on the - when we come around to AOS next time, that's thinking about giving you 4 more hours of sleep tonight.
Right now we're already down to 6 to 7 hours of sleep, and we figured after the long day today, it might be good to - if you want it, to have a longer sleep period tonight.
And what we'd do is just eat into the rest period down the line there.
We've got about two rev's there, you know, where we can eat into without any problem.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
I think, after today, tomorrow just can't be hard.
I'll tell you, these pressure suits, even in zero gravity, are something else.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What was that in zero gravity you said, John?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I said these pressure suits are something else, even in zero gravity.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
I can imagine that.
Well, listen, it'll - You won't be asleep before you come AOS next time, will you?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I probably won't sleep at all tonight.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: [Laughter.] Okay.

Well, what I'm getting at is, you can think about it, talk it over, and see if you'd like to do that, or if you want to now, we can go ahead and start building the Flight Plan around that, but if you want to do that, we can work on revising the Flight Plan while you're asleep tonight then.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Let me talk it over with my compatriots here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That'll be fine.
No rush; we got about 3 minutes and 45 seconds until AOS - until LOS, and we can catch you coming around the corner next time if you want.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Charlie Brown.
This is Houston.
We show about a minute and a half until LOS.
And we'll expect to hear from you coming around on the other side.
However, I will wait for a call from you.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: All right.
Thank you.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Okay, Charlie Brown.
We're just about to lose you.
We'll see you at 110:15.
That's about 46 minutes from now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And we have Loss Of Signal.
We'll be reacquiring Apollo 10 again in 46 minutes.
At that time the spacecraft will be in its 18th revolution of the Moon, and we expect that the crew will still be up.

They advise that they have a fair amount of activity to get the spacecraft ready for the sleep period, and to complete unsuiting and having their evening meal before beginning the sleep period, so we expect that we will hear from them again as they come back around on the other side of the Moon in the 18th revolution.
We have some additional information on that APS burn to depletion.
The burn began at 108 hours, 52 minutes, 2 seconds.
A total burn time was 3 minutes, 31 seconds and that gave us a Delta-V, a change in velocity of 3,799 feet per second for the LM ascent stage.
The burn was targeted to put the LM ascent stage into a solar orbit and we assume that that has been achieved.
We won't have a good idea, as to what orbit it has gone into until we've had a chance to get some tracking data on the LEM.

During the conversation with the crew, Capcom Joe Engle advised them that if they desired, we would be able to add four hours to their sleep cycle tonight to make up for the amount of time that we have already cut into that sleep period, and that we would make up the time line during sleep periods later on in the mission.
At 109 hours, 31 minutes; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Flight Plan page 3-68.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 110 hours, 15 minutes into the flight of Apollo 10.
We're less than a minute now until acquiring the spacecraft now in its 18th revolution of the Moon.
The crew is scheduled to be in their sleep period at this time.

However before we had Loss Of Signal on the previous revolution we gave Tom Stafford the option of adding four hours to the sleep period to make up for the time that has been lost due to the additional activities, and getting a little bit of a late start - actually finishing up late with some of the activities relating to the LM rendezvous and APS burn to depletion.
And Stafford advised that he would talk it over with his fellow crewmen and let us know when we had Acquisition Of Signal on this revolution.
So we anticipate that the crew will still be up and will probably be beginning their sleep period on this revolution.
We should have Acquisition Of Signal by now.
We will stand by for CapCom Joe Engle to put in a call to the crew.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston, Houston.
This is Apollo 10.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Apollo 10, this is Houston.
How are you guys doing?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I bet you thought we were sleeping.
We were just getting dressed for the occasion.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The crew was extremely pleased to take off their pressure suits.
They are a necessary evil, but far from comfortable, even in zero-gravity.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I got some dope for you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You go ahead with the dope.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay, Joe.
At a GET of 110:15, battery C read 37 volts; Pyro Batt A, 37; Pyro Batt B, 37; RCS ring [means quad] A says 60 percent; B is 78, Charlie is 72, and Delta in 67.
The canister change has been made.
The fans have been cycled.
And [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Joe, are you still there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger, Charlie Brown.
We're standing by.
We got all your readouts so far, all the way down to the fans cycled.
Have you anything more?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
We got a dosimeter reading: CDR is 26038, the CMP is 05308, and the LMP is 15040; and on the CDR, that was 26039.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We got all that, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And the crew status is tired, and happy, and hungry, and thirsty, and horny, and all those other things.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy everything, and we've solutions and pills for everything but item 4 [means 5].
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You're in trouble when I get back anyway.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: But we just didn't want you to forget what the good things are like back on Earth, Gene-o.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: How can I?
I keep looking at this Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
Roger.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We dumped the waste water, and we dropped it down to 20 - We dropped it down to 22 per cent, Joe.
But I guess that's all right, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
That's Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Now, what else can we do for you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, let's see, Gene-o.
You can give us a pill report, and I guess you haven't had time to take any today.
And, also, let us know if you've made the water taste bad [chlorinating the drinking water] for tomorrow.
And, also, you can zero the Command Module optics.
And I can't think of anything else right now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You want us to zero the Command Module optics.
We will chlorinate the water last thing, and we didn't take any pills yet.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We kind of figured that.
And, let me ask you about - Let me ask you about this proposed change for the Flight Plan for tomorrow, in other words, adding a couple of hours onto your sleep tonight.
Would - Did you guys get a chance to talk that over.
Do you want to do that?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I'll tell you.
Okay, Joe.
What time would that - how many hours would that get us up from - like, what's our proposed get-up time now.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Tom.
Your proposed get-up time is - Let's see.
It looks like 117:30, about.
And we'd add 2 hours onto that.
Okay.
I just got the word.
We can make that 3 to 4 hours, if we wanted to.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: How about standing by for one, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Tom.
We sure will.
In other words, right now you're looking at about 7 hours from now for wake-up time, which - I don't know how soon you're ready to go to sleep, but that would give you something like 6½ of sleep.
I'm guessing.
And we could add 4 hours on to that 3 to 4 hours, which would give you 9 to 10 hours of sleep.
That, incidentally, is not going to compr...
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay, Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
That, incidentally, will not compromise anything that we've got planned.
We just - We got some pad time on down in the Flight Plan as you know.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: All right, that was the rest period in the middle of the day, there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Joe.
This is Charlie Brown.
We think we'll take you up on that and sleep in for at least2 hours longer, which will give us about 9 hours.
I think we need it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We sure copy on that, Tom, and we'll go ahead and - I'll tell you what we'll do.
We'll go ahead and plan work a Flight Plan around giving you an extra 4 hours.
And if you want to crank up earlier, we'll see what we can do about that - cranking early in the morning then, because think you could probably use that rest, too.
You guys had a whale of a day, today.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
[Laughter.] That was quite a day.
You don't do that every day.

[Laughter.] And why don't we play it like that, so what - give us what your proposed wake-up time is; and, just like this morning we got up a little early, give us the hours for proposed wake-up time; we may beat that.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
Stand by just a second, Tom.
I'll get it for you here.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Charlie Brown, this is Houston.
Tom, you sound like you could use a fountain of vigor about now.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
Would you believe about two of them.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I don't know what you'd do with them after you got them though.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Just throw them up, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger that.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Can you uplink something like that, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes.
Could you uplink something like that?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We did our best in the Flight Plans and tool kits and stuff like that.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
We noticed that on the LRL, there.
Say, just wanted to ask you a question, too.
How did the TV look?
We haven't even had time to even think about it.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Say again.
How did the TV look you say?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Roger, how did the TV look during that station - official station keeping?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, That was outstanding today.
That was really good.
Really had a lot of good detail and man, that color, Tom Well, I don't know what to use for words, but you'll have to wait until you get back.
That really is going over.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
But you can really pick up the silver and the black and the flag and all that on the LEM, then?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
Let's see, I don't know that we picked up the LEM, but we sure got - Yes, the ascent stage was really great.
We could pick up the colors on it all right.
The Mylar showed up real good.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Joe.
Where do you suppose Snoopy is by now?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stand by.
I'll get a readout on that, Gene-o.
He's still sailing along.
I think - Let me check.
Yeah.
We're still tracking him.
Let me get some words on how far out he is.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: 10, just for your info, we show about 9.7 foot a second [2.95 m/s] separation, and we think it was just from that cabin venting on Snoopy after you'd separated.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
Well, you know he - up there's where our hatch has this insulation that's been bothering us, itching us, you know and stored in both cabins.
And when Snoopy took off, that insulation just exploded all over the place just like a snowstorm around the Moon.
And out of the midst of the snowstorm came Snoopy taking off.
[Laughter.]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Laughter.] He did?
[Laughter.] [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Houston, 10.
Did you say that Snoopy's cabin pressure went down to zero?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's affirmative, Tom.
It went all the way down.
Down to zero in 10 seconds, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Hey, Joe.
I went back in a second time to make sure that dump valve was in Auto, so it - Something must have happened, because it was in Auto.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes.
I copy.
It was probably that forward hatch you got in the Command Module with you.
That may have had something to do with it.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
[Laughter.] Sure.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Apollo 10.
Well, I guess Snoop performed real well with respect to the propulsion objectives that we had for it, didn't it, when you let it off?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger that.
He sure did, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Well, real good.
We got one heck of a lot of data today, that's for sure.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Boy, Roger that.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Joe, if you want a LEM simulation ride, with your two kids get - put a big - a big metal bowl on your head and beat on it with spoons.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: [Laughter.] Okay.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Joe, I guess I've flown well over a hundred different types of aircraft, and that made my third spacecraft; but of all of them, I've never heard anything as noisy as Snoopy.
It was too much.
Between the fans and the pumps and those thrusters firing on that thin skin, it was really a kick.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Laughter.] I'll bet it was.
You've just never been inside a dog when he is barking and kicking and scratching fleas all at the same time.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes, that's right.
This dog even wagged its tail a little bit on the ascent burn.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Laughter.] Yes.
Rog.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And he chased his tail on staging.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Rog.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You think that guy in the whale had a time.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Roger.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Houston, Apollo 10, we have one other question.
Just where did you propose that we stow that canister we brought back from the LM.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Tom.
The most logical place right now looks like it'll be in the sleep bag underneath your couch.
However, what we're going to do tomorrow is run an exercise over there in the - in the mockup, and figure out where the set place is with all the other gear you got on board.
We'll come up with several ideas and let you pick the one you like best.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You know, I can see what happens if that couch happens to stroke a little bit with that metal canister underneath it.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is referring the compression (stroking) of the crew couch support struts at splashdown.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Yes.
Well, I think it - If you keep it rolled up pretty close to your head there, up near the ORDEAL box, there, why the couch strokes down and toward the bottom, doesn't it.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
It's by our heads.
That may be okay.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And, Houston, Apollo 10.
We're coming right back over landing site 1 in all the places.
Say, it's just starting to look like we said before, NASA Road 1.
We can sure pick out every little crater now.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'll bet you can.
I'll bet it's looking pretty familiar by now, too, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
We're coming right up on landing site 1 here.
You can look straight ahead, and there's Maskelyne, Maskelyne B, lead up to [landmark tracking sites] 130, 129.
I have Moltke over on the left, and out there the plains, the Oklahoma Hills on the left, and the landing site.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Jack - Jack Schmitt's still here tonight.
He says you guys are overtrained, reading off names like that.
Hey, listen, Snoopy is about 6,000 miles [11,100 km] above you and still going, and we're still getting data on him.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Well, good.
That sounds great, sounds like you got some power left in those batteries.
Well, we're really glad to see you get all the data on the ascent burn.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Tom.
This wake-up now.
It looks like...
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: [Garble] miss her?.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead.
I'll wait.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Go ahead, Joe.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Tom.

On your wake-up in the morning - Well, on Snoopy first, they say we're expecting the power on those batteries to last until about 120 hours, so we got about another 10 hours-worth of tracking, it looks like.
And on your wake-up in the morning, what it looks like now, the best plan is to wake you up just before LOS on Rev 23, which would be at right about 121 hours.
And we'll give you a little data right away, enough to get you through the next REV, and that'll give you the back side to get woke up and dressed and break out some chow and stuff.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
So we're looking at about 121 hours.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's affirmative.
And we'll call you just as - just as late as we can and still get what data we need to up to you before LOS.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Real good.
Thank you.
We're right now just passing over - We're exactly over Maskelyne, and here's Maskelyne B up ahead.
And we've got the Sidewinder Rille over on the right.
We've got - here's Diamondback on the right, Sidewinder's on the left, and this whole chain of craters leading up to site 2.
And, again, if you didn't hear me, down below, there, it doesn't look near as rough and as rugged out here in the mare areas as it does in the other parts.
It's pretty well chained up.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Boy, that really sounds good, Tom.
Jack Schmitt is standing here, and he says that he's setting up some briefings when you guys get back.
This time you're going to be briefing him.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
And I think we can sure tell the difference between old and new craters, and the way it the site slips in, it was very obvious.

Those pictures came out, we'll show him some boulders and tell him that Censorinus A has some nice great big, both white and black, huge boulders on both the inside of the rim and quite a - well, most of them are on the outside of the rim, but it's pretty rugged country, and stay away from Censorinus A there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
We copy that.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Hey, Joe.
When this surface down here ceases to be interesting, it's time to bring us home.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We'll send up a replacement, Gene-o.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Well, right now it's still pretty inter - Well, right now it's still pretty interesting.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay.
We'll leave you up there a while yet.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: We just went over Moltke, and we got Sabine and Ritter right underneath us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger that.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: You might tell Jack that U.S.
1, when you get down close, comparing it to a runway from about 50,000 feet [15,200 meters] must be close to a thousand feet across.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
I'd say it...
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, Houston.
If Jack Schmitt's still there, we're passing over the crater.
We got it named after him.
It's right past Ritter and Sabine, and right here you can see some tremendous boulders down on the outside rim, there.
They're great big white ones.
I'd say they're, oh, to see it from this altitude here - and they got long shadows on them, they're at least about a hundred feet or more in diameter.
And down near the bottom, you can see where the sides are slumping in.
It's more like the tailings off a mine.
And the sides are white and gray.
You can see fractured structure in there, too.
We got some pictures of it.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Very good.
We copied all that, Tom.
Thank you.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And right now, we're still just looking at U.S.
1 as it disappears over into the terminator.
That's about [garble].
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, Tom, speaking of the cameras, do you have any - any of those camera problems you want us to try and work on tonight?
It sounded like you had some problems other than film packs.
Is there anything that we can help you out with, trying to figure out tonight?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Joe, those were preflight problems.
And the main thing, the packs we can see werent fitted to the camera and run through.
And the batteries on them, my Hasselblad, went dead just as I got to the site.
I hope I got some pictures of it.
I got all the approaches into it.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Very good.
Understand, Tom.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And tell Jack tomorrow, we're going to get him a picture of this, because U.S.
1 suddenly jumps sideways up here.
And maybe that's the strike slip fault he's been looking for, but it suddenly jumped sideways and you can see it, or else maybe it's just another one has gone into it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: And then it fades out.
But it's been elevated in certain areas here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
That's good to hear.
And on the cameras, Tom, if you run into a problem tomorrow, or you want both Hasselblads available.
Jack says you can put one battery in each camera, and it should operate okay.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yeah.
Okay.
We'll give that a try in the morning.
We'll be all ready to go after them and what we're doing now is we're passing the terminator.
We're going to go ahead and sack out tonight.
It's been a long day, and we're just watching, still loving to watch the moonscape go by and observing here as we go over to the terminator.
And we'll be talking to you tomorrow morning.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
Mighty fine, Tom.
That sounds good, and we'll talk to you some more about data and stuff in the morning.

On your LCL recovery checklist, before you backpack that stuff all away, tomorrow we'll just go through them, and you can just call down the item numbers and let me know where you have them stowed so we can work out your c.g.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Will do.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And I guess that's about it.
We want you to know you guys did one whale of a job today.
You really did us all proud.
The big troops on the back row walked out of here shaking their heads and grinning from ear to ear.
They could have eaten a banana sideways and never touched it.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: [Laughter.] Well, great, that makes us real happy.
It was a heck of a workload.
One thing I wanted to check on.
It looked like on board, Joe, that the landing radar did a great job on locking at a pretty good altitude and performing all the way through.
Have you got any word on that yet?
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
That agrees with what we were looking at down here, Tom.
It looked like it performed just real well.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
Now one reason I wasn't able to hold that right on exactly 10 degrees, but was off a few tenths, and even up to 1 degree, the rate needles on the attitude error indicator weren't calibrated.
So when I had a zero pitch rate, actually, it ended up at the end with that calibrator, just before docking, it was three-tenths of a degree off.
And I was trying to just eyeball that and eyeball the DSKY, but I think we got what we wanted was in the local horizontal reference there.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
We copied all that.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes.
Also, just a couple of more comments.
It was a real ride, that ascent engine was; I guess we had the longest burn on it to date, and it takes you on quite a little pitch and yaw excursion there as you take off.
I mean it continues on, you know, the way, just - with a non-gimballing engine, but yet it burned out beautifully on residuals, but you're really hiccupping back and forth in that bear.
It was quite a ride for 15 seconds.
Over.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger.
I'll bet.
I'll bet it got pretty sporty there towards - You had a pretty light vehicle there, didn't you?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Oh, yes.
Just one pulse in PGNS, you go bang, and it really takes off.
Also, the vehicle's so light that you noticed all the structure shaking when you fired pulse.
And it sounded just like you'd awake inside of a rainwater tub with somebody beating on it with a bongo drum.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Laughter.] Is that right?
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Yes, It's quite a machine.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Tom, this is Houston.
We've been talking with the doctor, and it sounds like there's only one way that we can get you unwound and to sleep tonight.
We're not sure how to get that up to you.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: [Laughter.] Yes.
Understand.
Understand, Joe.
Well, we're going to sack out shortly.
But I say, after a day like that, we just want to talk about a few things there and relax.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Yes.
We sure understand.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hello, Houston.
Charlie [garble] just a little switching around over here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
We're still reading you five-by, Gene-o.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Okay.
I'll be listening to you tonight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene, very good.
And we'll try not to bother you.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Don't feel bad if you have something though.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Barb called over just a few minutes ago.
She stayed up right to the end listening, and she was happy as could be.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Beautiful.
Appreciate that.
[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes.
We've been keeping in pretty close touch with all the gals, in fact, for all three of you guys.
And those dang gals are running in to read the Flight Plans and the checklists, and they keep asking us when you're going to do this and why you didn't do that.
And they come up with some pretty embarrassing questions sometimes.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: Joe, we got enough of those people.
We don't need any more.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You've got three of them waiting when you get back.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: I guess we'll take it in stride [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Engle

tokens: This is Apollo Control.
We have about 1 minute, 15 seconds now before we lose contact with Apollo 10 as it goes around behind the Moon on this the 18th revolution.
Flight Director Milton Windler has gone around the room and checked the status of the spacecraft with all of his flight controllers before we lose contact.
Everything appears to be in good order at this time.
The flight surgeon advises that the crew appears to be either asleep or going to sleep at this time, and we don't expect that we'll hear any more from them for the rest of the sleep period.
They do have the option to extend this sleep period through 121 hours Ground Elapsed Time if they so desire.
Tom Stafford said that they may be up and about somewhat before that, if they're not up by that time, the ground would awake them.
And we should have Loss Of Signal just about now.
And our EECOM advises that we do have LOS.

We'll be prepared to come up again when we reacquire contact with the spacecraft although I don't expect we'll have anything more than telemetry information at that time, which will be about 46 minutes or so from now.
At 111 hours, 28 minutes; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO
